h1Title: Lymphoma: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Lymphoma is a cancer of lymphocytes — the white blood cells of the lymphatic system — that originates in lymph nodes, the spleen, bone marrow, or other lymphoid tissues. Two broad categories exist: Hodgkin lymphoma (HL) and non-Hodgkin lymphoma (NHL), which encompass more than 80 distinct subtypes. The American Cancer Society estimated 89,310 new lymphoma diagnoses in the United States in 2023. Hodgkin lymphoma, defined by the presence of Reed-Sternberg cells, is one of the most curable cancers — over 80% of patients are cured with first-line chemotherapy. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma ranges from extremely indolent forms (follicular lymphoma) to highly aggressive subtypes (diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, Burkitt lymphoma). Treatment has been transformed by the anti-CD20 antibody rituximab, newer immunotherapy agents, and CAR-T cell therapy.

keyStats.prevalence: Approximately 89,310 new US cases in 2023: 8,830 HL, 80,470 NHL (American Cancer Society 2023)
keyStats.demographics: HL: bimodal age distribution with peaks at ages 20-34 and over 55; NHL: incidence rises with age, median age at NHL diagnosis approximately 67
keyStats.avgAge: Hodgkin lymphoma median age at diagnosis: 39; Non-Hodgkin lymphoma median age: 67
keyStats.globalCases: Approximately 544,000 new NHL cases and 83,000 new HL cases globally in 2022 (GLOBOCAN)

definition: Lymphoma (ICD-10: C81-C88) is a malignant neoplasm of B lymphocytes, T lymphocytes, or natural killer (NK) cells that accumulates in lymphoid tissue. Hodgkin lymphoma (ICD-10: C81) is distinguished by pathognomonic Reed-Sternberg cells (large binucleated CD30+/CD15+ cells) surrounded by a reactive inflammatory infiltrate; it is predominantly B-cell derived and arises most commonly in the cervical, mediastinal, or para-aortic lymph nodes. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma (ICD-10: C82-C88) encompasses a spectrum from the most common adult subtype — diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL, 25-30% of NHL) — to follicular lymphoma (FL, 20%), mantle cell lymphoma, marginal zone lymphoma, Burkitt lymphoma, peripheral T-cell lymphomas, and many others. Lymphoma is clinically staged using the Lugano modification of the Ann Arbor system (stage I: single lymph node region or extranodal site; stage II: two or more regions on the same side of the diaphragm; stage III: regions on both sides; stage IV: disseminated extranodal disease) and assessed with PET-CT in FDG-avid subtypes. Hematology-oncology manages all subtypes; bone marrow transplant teams are involved for relapsed or refractory disease.

typesClassification.description: Four subtypes: nodular sclerosis (70%), mixed cellularity (20-25%), lymphocyte-rich, and lymphocyte-depleted. All defined by Reed-Sternberg cells. Highly curable with ABVD or BV-AVD chemotherapy; PET-adapted treatment de-escalation reduces long-term toxicity.
Rare (5% of HL); CD20-positive lymphocytic and histiocytic (popcorn) cells rather than classic Reed-Sternberg cells. Indolent behavior; may respond to rituximab monotherapy in early stages.

Most common aggressive NHL; treated with R-CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone). Curative in 60-70% of patients with first-line therapy. Molecular subtypes (GCB vs. non-GCB; double-hit/triple-hit) define prognosis.

Second most common NHL; an indolent B-cell lymphoma with a median survival over 15 years. Rituximab-based therapy controls disease but does not cure most patients. Grade 3B and transformation to DLBCL require aggressive therapy.

Mantle cell lymphoma (MCL, treated with BTK inhibitors ± chemotherapy), Burkitt lymphoma (highly aggressive, curative with intensive chemotherapy), marginal zone lymphoma, peripheral T-cell lymphomas, and cutaneous lymphomas require distinct approaches.

primarySymptoms: Painless, rubbery, firm swelling of one or more lymph nodes, most commonly in the neck, axilla, or groin — the hallmark presenting finding in both HL and NHL.
Drenching night sweats requiring a change of clothing or bedding, occurring on more than half of nights, one of the three B symptoms defined in lymphoma staging.
Unexplained fever above 38°C occurring on more than half of nights for at least 1 month without an infectious cause — a classic B symptom that worsens prognosis.
Unintentional weight loss of more than 10% body weight over 6 months, the third B symptom, associated with advanced disease and poorer outcomes.
Mediastinal mass causing dry cough, shortness of breath, or superior vena cava syndrome (facial and arm swelling, dilated neck veins) — particularly common in nodular sclerosis HL.
Pruritus (generalized skin itching without rash) in up to 25% of Hodgkin lymphoma patients, of unclear pathogenesis but potentially severe.
Splenomegaly causing left-sided abdominal fullness, early satiety, or left shoulder tip pain from capsular stretch or infarction.
Fatigue and pallor disproportionate to mild anemia — hemolytic anemia occurs in some lymphoma subtypes; marrow infiltration contributes in stage IV disease.
Abdominal pain, distension, or obstructive symptoms from bulky mesenteric or retroperitoneal nodal disease, more common in NHL than HL.
Rapidly growing painful lymph nodes over 1-2 weeks suggesting transformation of an indolent lymphoma to DLBCL or Burkitt lymphoma — a lymphomatous emergency.
earlyWarningSigns: Painless neck lymphadenopathy persisting more than 4-6 weeks in a young adult without recent infection
Persistent mediastinal widening on chest X-ray in a patient without pulmonary symptoms — prompts CT imaging
New B symptoms (night sweats, fever, weight loss) in any patient with known lymphadenopathy regardless of prior biopsy results
Incidentally detected splenomegaly on abdominal imaging prompting lymphoma staging workup
Unexplained elevated LDH and beta-2-microglobulin on routine biochemistry in a patient with lymphadenopathy
emergencySigns: Superior vena cava (SVC) syndrome with respiratory compromise, stridor, or cyanosis — requires urgent dexamethasone and oncology consultation
Spinal cord compression from epidural lymphomatous mass causing progressive leg weakness and bladder dysfunction — urgent MRI and neurosurgical assessment
Tumor lysis syndrome: hyperkalemia, hyperphosphatemia, and acute renal failure after chemotherapy initiation for Burkitt or DLBCL — monitor electrolytes every 4-6 hours
Acute upper airway obstruction or respiratory failure from bulky mediastinal lymphoma in HL — requires urgent corticosteroids before definitive biopsy in extremis
Rapidly progressive central nervous system symptoms from CNS lymphoma or lymphomatous meningitis — requires urgent brain MRI and cerebrospinal fluid analysis

causes.description: HIV infection increases NHL risk 60-100× and is associated with aggressive B-cell lymphomas (DLBCL, Burkitt, primary CNS lymphoma). Solid-organ transplant recipients on calcineurin inhibitors have a 30-50× elevated risk of post-transplant lymphoproliferative disorder (PTLD). Autoimmune diseases (rheumatoid arthritis, Sjögren syndrome, lupus) elevate NHL risk 2-5×.

EBV is present in 30-50% of classical HL Reed-Sternberg cells and is the direct cause of Burkitt lymphoma in endemic African regions and primary CNS lymphoma in immunocompromised patients. EBV-related lymphomas arise in immunosuppressed individuals and account for the majority of post-transplant lymphomas.

H. pylori is the causative agent of gastric mucosa-associated lymphoid tissue (MALT) lymphoma; antibiotic eradication of H. pylori produces complete remission in 75-80% of localized gastric MALT lymphoma. The mechanism involves chronic antigen-driven B-cell proliferation in the gastric mucosa.

Hepatitis C virus is associated with splenic marginal zone lymphoma and cryoglobulinemia-related B-cell lymphoma; antiviral therapy with direct-acting antivirals can produce lymphoma remission. HTLV-1 causes adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma in 3-5% of carriers. Borrelia burgdorferi and Chlamydia psittaci are implicated in specific cutaneous lymphoma subtypes.

Alkylating agents and radiation therapy for prior cancers increase the risk of secondary NHL and T-cell lymphomas, typically presenting 5-15 years post-treatment. Radiation for HL, particularly mediastinal irradiation, elevates the long-term risk of secondary breast cancer, lung cancer, and cardiovascular disease.

First-degree relatives of NHL patients have approximately 2× the population risk. Genome-wide association studies have identified susceptibility loci at HLA-A, HLA-DQA1, and IRF4 for HL; multiple loci for specific NHL subtypes. Inherited ATM mutations and other DNA-repair gene variants predispose to certain T- and B-cell lymphomas.

riskFactors.description: Effective antiretroviral therapy substantially reduces HIV-associated NHL risk but does not eliminate it. Lymphoma risk is highest with CD4 count below 100 cells/µL. Pre-exposure prophylaxis and early ART initiation are protective.

riskFactors.factor: Solid organ or stem cell transplantation
riskFactors.description: Immunosuppressive therapy post-transplant elevates NHL risk 30-50×. Risk is highest in the first year and with EBV-seropositive donor/seronegative recipient pairings. Reduction of immunosuppression is first-line treatment for PTLD.
Persistent H. pylori gastritis is a necessary precursor of most gastric MALT lymphoma. H. pylori eradication is curative in approximately 75-80% of stage I-II gastric MALT.

Sjögren syndrome carries a 15-20× elevated risk of parotid or marginal zone lymphoma. Rheumatoid arthritis and lupus carry 2-5× elevated NHL risk, partly attributable to disease activity and partly to methotrexate or immunosuppressive therapy.
NHL incidence rises steeply with age; 50% of NHL patients are over 65. Older patients have worse outcomes with standard R-CHOP, partly due to anthracycline cardiotoxicity and dose reductions.

Organophosphates and chlorinated herbicides (including glyphosate, though evidence remains contested) show associations with NHL risk in meta-analyses of agricultural workers, with RR approximately 1.3-1.7.
Familial aggregation is well-documented in CLL and follicular lymphoma. Genome-wide association studies have identified multiple low-penetrance susceptibility alleles.

affectedDemographics: Hodgkin lymphoma: bimodal distribution, with the first peak in young adults (20-34) and a second peak in individuals over 55; more common in men and in high-income countries.
NHL: predominantly a disease of older adults; median age 67; slight male predominance; incidence 3-fold higher in immunocompromised individuals.
DLBCL: more common in Black patients (by age-adjusted incidence) than White patients in the US (SEER data).
Burkitt lymphoma: endemic form (EBV-associated) peaks at ages 4-7 in equatorial Africa; sporadic form in Western countries predominantly affects adults.
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis requires a lymph node excision biopsy or core needle biopsy providing adequate tissue for histology, immunohistochemistry (IHC), and molecular studies — fine-needle aspiration alone is insufficient for lymphoma classification. The minimum IHC panel for B-cell lymphoma includes CD20, CD3, CD10, BCL2, BCL6, MYC, Ki-67, and cyclin D1 (for mantle cell lymphoma); T-cell panels add CD4, CD8, CD30, and ALK. FISH for MYC, BCL2, and BCL6 rearrangements identifies double-hit/triple-hit DLBCL, which requires intensive regimens beyond standard R-CHOP. Staging uses PET-CT (18F-FDG) for FDG-avid lymphomas (HL, DLBCL, FL grades 1-3A) and CT with bone marrow biopsy for low-grade NHL subtypes. PET-CT detects occult stage IV disease not identified by CT in 10-20% of HL patients and guides interim response assessment after 2-4 cycles — Deauville 5-point scale: scores 1-3 indicate response, 4-5 indicate residual disease. For CNS lymphoma or suspected CNS involvement, brain MRI with gadolinium and CSF cytology plus flow cytometry are required. Serology for HIV, HBsAg/HBcAb (rituximab can reactivate hepatitis B), HCV, and HTLV-1 is obtained before treatment. LDH and beta-2-microglobulin are prognostic biomarkers.

diagnosticTests.test: Excisional or core needle lymph node biopsy
diagnosticTests.purpose: Provides adequate tissue for histology, IHC, FISH, and molecular studies required for lymphoma classification
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Excisional biopsy is outpatient surgery under local anesthesia or sedation; a whole lymph node is removed. Core needle biopsy uses a large-bore needle under CT or ultrasound guidance. Full results including molecular studies take 7-14 days.

diagnosticTests.test: PET-CT (18F-FDG) for staging and response assessment
diagnosticTests.purpose: Whole-body metabolic staging; identifies nodal and extranodal disease; guides biopsy of the most metabolically active site; assesses interim and end-of-treatment response (Deauville scale)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Fasting 4-6 hours before; radiotracer injected IV; 60-minute wait; 30-minute scan. Results within 24-48 hours interpreted using the Deauville 5-point scale.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Detects marrow involvement (stage IV); now replaced by PET-CT in most FDG-avid lymphomas but remains required in FL, MCL, and NHL subtypes not well visualized by PET
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Needle inserted into posterior iliac crest under local anesthesia; mild to moderate pain lasting 30-60 minutes. Histology and flow cytometry results within 5-10 days.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Classifies lymphoma subtype (B vs. T cell, specific entity) and identifies prognostic markers (Ki-67 proliferation index, BCL2 and MYC overexpression)
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on biopsy tissue; no additional sample needed. Results in 5-10 days. Multiple antibodies applied to tissue sections and interpreted by hematopathologist.

diagnosticTests.test: FISH for MYC, BCL2, and BCL6 rearrangements
diagnosticTests.purpose: Identifies double-hit (MYC + BCL2 or BCL6) and triple-hit DLBCL, which require DA-R-EPOCH rather than R-CHOP
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed on biopsy tissue. Results in 7-10 days. A fluorescent probe hybridizes to specific chromosomal loci; rearrangements indicate high-grade disease.

diagnosticTests.test: Brain MRI with gadolinium + CSF analysis
diagnosticTests.purpose: Evaluates for CNS involvement in high-risk DLBCL (IPI score ≥4, double-hit, involvement of testis, kidney, or adrenal gland) and diagnoses primary CNS lymphoma
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: MRI takes 30-45 minutes; gadolinium contrast injected IV. Lumbar puncture for CSF cytology and flow cytometry takes 15-20 minutes under local anesthesia; headache possible for 24-48 hours afterward.

treatmentOverview: Treatment is subtype- and stage-specific. Hodgkin lymphoma: early-stage (I-IIA) favorable disease is treated with ABVD (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine) × 2 cycles plus involved-site radiation therapy (ISRT) 20 Gy, with cure rates exceeding 95%. Interim PET-adapted strategies allow omission of radiation in PET-negative patients (AHL2011 trial). Unfavorable or advanced HL uses ABVD × 6 or BV-AVD (brentuximab vedotin substituting for bleomycin), with BV-AVD showing superior 5-year progression-free survival (82.1% vs 75.3%, ECHELON-1). Pembrolizumab and nivolumab are active in relapsed/refractory HL. DLBCL first-line: R-CHOP (rituximab 375 mg/m², cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone) every 21 days for 6 cycles achieves cure in 60-70%. Double-hit DLBCL (MYC + BCL2/BCL6 rearrangements) is treated with DA-R-EPOCH (dose-adjusted) due to inferior outcomes with R-CHOP. Polatuzumab vedotin-piiq added to R-CHP (pola-R-CHP) improves EFS vs R-CHOP (POLARIX trial: 2-year EFS 76.7% vs 70.2%) and is now an approved first-line option for previously untreated DLBCL. CNS prophylaxis with high-dose methotrexate or intrathecal chemotherapy is given to high-risk DLBCL. Follicular lymphoma stage I-II: wait-and-watch or rituximab × 4 weekly doses ± radiation. Stage III-IV symptomatic FL uses rituximab-based induction (R-CVP, R-CHOP, or R-bendamustine) followed by 2 years of rituximab maintenance, which improves progression-free but not overall survival. Relapsed/refractory aggressive lymphoma: CAR-T cell therapy (axicabtagene ciloleucel, tisagenlecleucel, lisocabtagene maraleucel) achieves long-term remission in 30-40% of patients failing salvage chemotherapy (ZUMA-7, TRANSFORM, PILOT trials).

medicalTreatments.name: ABVD (doxorubicin, bleomycin, vinblastine, dacarbazine)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard first-line chemotherapy for Hodgkin lymphoma. Four 28-day cycles for early favorable HL; six cycles for advanced HL. Bleomycin pulmonary toxicity is the main long-term concern; PET-guided omission after cycle 2 negative scan reduces bleomycin exposure.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 5-year PFS approximately 87% in early-stage HL; 5-year OS 85-90% in advanced HL (HD18, AHL2011 trials)

medicalTreatments.name: BV-AVD (brentuximab vedotin, doxorubicin, vinblastine, dacarbazine)
medicalTreatments.description: Brentuximab vedotin (anti-CD30 antibody-drug conjugate) replaces bleomycin in ABVD. Preferred in stage III-IV HL especially in patients with pulmonary risk factors. G-CSF given with every cycle to prevent febrile neutropenia.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 5-year PFS 82.1% vs 75.3% for ABVD; OS benefit at 5 years 87.5% vs 83.3% (ECHELON-1 trial)

medicalTreatments.name: R-CHOP (rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, prednisone)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard first-line treatment for DLBCL and most aggressive B-cell NHL. Delivered every 21 days for 6 cycles. Rituximab targets CD20 on B cells, enhancing cytotoxic kill.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 6-year OS 62-72% in DLBCL; cure in approximately 60-70% of patients

medicalTreatments.name: Polatuzumab vedotin plus R-CHP (pola-R-CHP)
medicalTreatments.description: Anti-CD79b antibody-drug conjugate combined with rituximab, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, and prednisone; vincristine replaced by polatuzumab. FDA-approved first-line option for previously untreated DLBCL.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: 2-year event-free survival 76.7% vs 70.2% for R-CHOP; benefit concentrated in high-IPI and non-GCB subtypes (POLARIX trial)

medicalTreatments.name: R-bendamustine (rituximab plus bendamustine)
medicalTreatments.description: Preferred first-line induction for indolent NHL (follicular lymphoma, marginal zone lymphoma, MCL in selected patients). Better tolerated and at least as effective as R-CHOP in indolent histotypes.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Median PFS 69.5 months vs 31.2 months for R-CHOP in indolent NHL (BRIGHT study); ORR 97%

medicalTreatments.name: CAR-T cell therapy (axicabtagene ciloleucel, tisagenlecleucel, lisocabtagene maraleucel)
medicalTreatments.description: CD19-directed CAR-T cells for relapsed/refractory DLBCL after ≥2 prior lines or as second-line therapy in primary-refractory or early-relapse disease (within 12 months). One-time infusion; cytokine release syndrome and neurotoxicity are key adverse events.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Long-term remission in 30-40% of relapsed/refractory DLBCL; superior EFS vs salvage chemo + ASCT in primary-refractory disease (ZUMA-7: 4-year EFS 41.5% vs 17.2%)

surgicalOptions.name: Autologous stem cell transplantation (ASCT)
surgicalOptions.description: Standard consolidation for relapsed/refractory DLBCL and HL responding to salvage chemotherapy. High-dose conditioning chemotherapy followed by infusion of patient's own previously harvested stem cells. Role increasingly challenged by CAR-T therapy in DLBCL.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 3-year PFS for relapsed HL post-ASCT approximately 55-65%; for DLBCL 35-50% (CORAL study)

surgicalOptions.name: Allogeneic stem cell transplantation
surgicalOptions.description: Used in selected relapsed/refractory cases, particularly T-cell lymphomas and patients with failed CAR-T therapy. Provides graft-versus-lymphoma effect. Higher procedure-related mortality (10-20%) than ASCT limits use to younger fit patients.
surgicalOptions.successRate: 5-year OS 40-50% for relapsed aggressive lymphoma in experienced centers; significantly lower with multiple prior relapses

alternativeTreatments.name: Watchful waiting for asymptomatic indolent lymphoma
alternativeTreatments.description: Up to 50% of patients with stage III-IV follicular lymphoma meet criteria for observation at diagnosis (no B symptoms, no bulky disease, normal LDH, no marrow compromise). Randomized trials (BNLI, NCIC CTG) show no survival detriment vs. early treatment initiation.

alternativeTreatments.name: Involved-site radiation therapy (ISRT) for early-stage lymphoma
alternativeTreatments.description: Radiation to the involved lymph node regions only, replacing older extended-field RT. Used as consolidation after 2-4 cycles ABVD for early HL; also curative for early-stage FL and MALT lymphoma. Minimizes long-term radiation toxicity to adjacent organs.

whySeeSpecialist: Lymphoma diagnosis requires a hematopathologist with expertise in lymphoma classification, and treatment selection is highly subtype-specific — incorrect classification leads to under- or over-treatment. Relapsed or refractory cases require evaluation at a transplant center or CAR-T-certified program.
doctorSelectionGuide: Choose a hematologist-oncologist with a dedicated lymphoma practice at a comprehensive cancer center. For relapsed or refractory DLBCL, verify that the center is certified to administer CAR-T therapies and has an experienced toxicity management team for cytokine release syndrome.
hospitalCriteria: Rapid-access hematopathology with IHC and FISH panel within 7-10 days
Autologous and allogeneic stem cell transplant program
CAR-T cell therapy certification and management infrastructure
keyFacilities: Major academic medical centers with dedicated lymphoma programs
preventionStrategies: Treat HIV with antiretroviral therapy promptly; suppressed viral load reduces NHL risk substantially.
Eradicate Helicobacter pylori if detected in the setting of gastric MALT lymphoma or persistent unexplained dyspepsia with lymphoid hyperplasia on gastric biopsy.
Minimize immunosuppressive therapy in transplant recipients to the minimum required; use EBV viral load monitoring to detect early PTLD.
Limit occupational exposure to herbicides, pesticides, and organic solvents where possible.
Treat hepatitis C with direct-acting antiviral therapy; HCV-associated marginal zone lymphoma often responds to antiviral cure without chemotherapy.
lifestyleModifications: Complete rituximab-based and chemotherapy regimens on schedule — dose reductions and delays compromise cure rates.
Report fever above 38°C during neutropenic phases immediately; febrile neutropenia is a medical emergency.
Avoid live vaccines for at least 6 months after rituximab and during any immunosuppressive therapy.
Receive annual inactivated influenza vaccine and pneumococcal vaccines once immune reconstitution allows.
Seek psychosocial support early; depression affects 20-30% of lymphoma patients and is associated with treatment non-adherence.

recommended: High-protein, high-calorie foods during chemotherapy to maintain weight and support immune recovery
Cooked or heat-treated foods during neutropenic periods to prevent foodborne infection
Folate-rich vegetables and B12 sources to support hematopoiesis after treatment
Adequate fluid intake (2-3 L/day) to facilitate clearance of chemotherapy metabolites
avoid: Raw fish, shellfish, and unpasteurized products during immunosuppression
Alcohol during active treatment; moderate alcohol may be acceptable after sustained remission
Grapefruit with CYP3A4-metabolized agents including cyclophosphamide and venetoclax
High-dose antioxidant supplements during radiotherapy, which may reduce radiation-induced free radical tumor cell kill

exerciseGuidelines: Moderate aerobic exercise (30 minutes, 3-5 days per week) during and after treatment reduces fatigue, depression, and lymphedema. Avoid vigorous activity with platelet count below 50,000/µL or during febrile neutropenia. Gradual return to normal exercise is safe after ASCT once engraftment is confirmed.
dailyManagement: Track fever twice daily during chemotherapy cycles; contact the oncology team for temperatures above 38°C.
Keep a record of lymph node sizes by palpation to monitor for new enlargement between imaging studies.
Take anti-nausea premedications as prescribed before each chemotherapy infusion day.
Attend scheduled interim and end-of-treatment PET-CT scans; these guide ongoing therapy decisions.
Maintain good hand and oral hygiene; neutropenic patients are at high risk of bacterial infections from oral flora.
prognosis: Prognosis depends heavily on subtype and stage. Hodgkin lymphoma overall: 5-year OS approximately 86% across all stages (SEER data). Early-stage favorable HL: cure rate over 95%. Advanced-stage HL: 5-year OS approximately 78% with modern BV-AVD or ABVD-based regimens. DLBCL: 60-70% cure with first-line R-CHOP; International Prognostic Index (IPI) 0-1 carries 5-year OS above 80%, IPI 4-5 approximately 50%. Double-hit DLBCL 5-year OS approximately 35-40% with DA-R-EPOCH. Follicular lymphoma: median overall survival exceeds 15-20 years; transformed FL carries a 5-year OS of approximately 50%. Burkitt lymphoma treated with intensive chemotherapy in young fit patients: cure rate approximately 70-90%. Mantle cell lymphoma: median OS 5-7 years; younger patients achieving MRD-negative remission after intensive induction and transplant achieve 10-year OS exceeding 50%.
recoveryTimeline: R-CHOP cycle: 21-day cycle, nadir at days 7-14, recovery by day 21. ABVD for HL: two cycles per month; most patients return to normal activity between cycles. CAR-T cell therapy: hospitalization for 2-3 weeks for infusion and acute toxicity management; immune reconstitution over 6-12 months. ASCT: 2-4 weeks inpatient; full recovery 3-6 months.
complications: Febrile neutropenia during chemotherapy: occurs in 10-25% of R-CHOP cycles without G-CSF; requires immediate IV antibiotics and hospitalization.
Anthracycline cardiotoxicity from cumulative doxorubicin: risk of cardiomyopathy above 400 mg/m² cumulative dose; requires baseline and follow-up echocardiography.

Secondary malignancies: radiation therapy for HL raises long-term risk of breast cancer, lung cancer, and coronary artery disease; anthracycline plus alkylating agents elevate secondary AML risk 1-2%.
Transformation of indolent NHL (follicular, marginal zone) to aggressive DLBCL: occurs at 2-3% per year; presents as rapidly growing mass, rising LDH, and B symptoms.
Hepatitis B reactivation with rituximab: can cause fulminant hepatitis; prevented by antiviral prophylaxis (entecavir or tenofovir) in HBsAg+ or HBcAb+ patients.

Cytokine release syndrome (CRS) and immune effector cell-associated neurotoxicity syndrome (ICANS) after CAR-T therapy: grade 3-4 CRS in 5-10%, ICANS in 10-20% of patients receiving axicabtagene ciloleucel.

supportResources.name: Leukemia and Lymphoma Society (LLS)
supportResources.description: Comprehensive lymphoma education, patient navigation services, financial assistance, and clinical trial matching.

supportResources.name: Lymphoma Coalition
supportResources.description: Global network of lymphoma patient groups providing peer support, education, and advocacy resources.

supportResources.name: Lymphoma Research Foundation
supportResources.description: US non-profit funding lymphoma research; offers a helpline, educational webinars, and patient resources for specific lymphoma subtypes.

confusedWithConditions.name: Leukemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Lymphoma originates in lymph nodes and lymphoid tissue and is diagnosed by lymph node biopsy; leukemia originates in bone marrow and is diagnosed by peripheral blood and marrow examination. Both can involve bone marrow in advanced stages. In CLL and lymphocytic lymphoma the distinction is largely anatomical — the same clonal B cells in the blood constitute CLL and in lymph nodes constitute small lymphocytic lymphoma (SLL).

confusedWithConditions.name: Anemia
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Anemia often accompanies lymphoma through marrow infiltration, autoimmune hemolysis, or treatment-related suppression, but anemia alone has many other causes. Lymphoma is the diagnosis when anemia co-occurs with lymphadenopathy, B symptoms, elevated LDH, or abnormal lymphocytes on blood smear.

confusedWithConditions.name: Tuberculosis
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Tuberculous lymphadenitis causes cervical and mediastinal lymphadenopathy in endemic regions and can exactly mimic lymphoma on imaging. Definitive distinction requires biopsy showing granulomatous inflammation with acid-fast bacilli, positive culture, or TB PCR, compared with Reed-Sternberg cells or monomorphic lymphoid infiltrate in lymphoma.

relatedConditions.name: Leukemia
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares the same hematology-oncology specialty; some entities (CLL/SLL) straddle both categories; treatment modalities including CAR-T and stem cell transplant overlap.

relatedConditions.name: Mesothelioma
relatedConditions.relevance: Both can present with mediastinal or pleural disease requiring biopsy for distinction; radiation therapy for mediastinal lymphoma elevates long-term pleural mesothelioma risk in occupational asbestos co-exposure.

relatedConditions.name: Breast Cancer
relatedConditions.relevance: Prior mediastinal radiotherapy for HL significantly elevates breast cancer risk in women; post-HL breast cancer surveillance is standard practice.

relatedConditions.name: Hepatitis C
relatedConditions.relevance: HCV drives marginal zone and diffuse large B-cell lymphoma; antiviral cure can produce lymphoma remission, making HCV testing before lymphoma treatment essential.

faqs.question: What is lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Lymphoma is a cancer of lymphocytes (white blood cells) that begins in lymph nodes or other lymphoid tissue. It includes Hodgkin lymphoma and over 80 types of non-Hodgkin lymphoma. The most common type in adults is diffuse large B-cell lymphoma; the most common in young adults is Hodgkin lymphoma.

faqs.question: What are the first signs of lymphoma?
faqs.answer: The most common first sign is painless swelling of lymph nodes, usually in the neck, armpit, or groin. Other early signs include night sweats, unexplained fever, and unintentional weight loss — the three B symptoms. Fatigue, itching, and mediastinal fullness also occur in Hodgkin lymphoma.

faqs.question: Is lymphoma curable?
faqs.answer: Hodgkin lymphoma is cured in over 80% of patients, making it one of the most curable cancers. Diffuse large B-cell lymphoma (DLBCL) is cured in 60-70% with R-CHOP chemotherapy. Follicular lymphoma is not considered curable with current therapies but has a median survival exceeding 15-20 years.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Hodgkin and non-Hodgkin lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Hodgkin lymphoma is defined by the presence of Reed-Sternberg cells and is highly curable with chemotherapy. Non-Hodgkin lymphoma encompasses over 80 subtypes of B-cell and T-cell malignancies with widely varying behavior and treatment. NHL accounts for 90% of all lymphoma cases.

faqs.question: What causes lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Lymphoma arises from mutations in lymphocytes. Known causes include immune suppression (HIV, transplant drugs), viral infections (EBV, HTLV-1, hepatitis C), and Helicobacter pylori for gastric MALT lymphoma. Most cases have no single identifiable cause.

faqs.question: How is lymphoma diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Diagnosis requires a lymph node biopsy with histology and immunohistochemistry. Fine-needle aspiration is insufficient. After diagnosis, PET-CT staging determines disease extent. Additional tests including FISH and bone marrow biopsy classify the specific subtype and guide treatment.

faqs.question: Do swollen lymph nodes always mean lymphoma?
faqs.answer: No. Swollen lymph nodes are most commonly caused by infections such as strep throat, mononucleosis, or viral illness. Lymphoma is suspected when lymph nodes remain swollen for more than 4-6 weeks without an obvious infectious cause, or when accompanied by night sweats, fever, or weight loss.

faqs.question: What is the survival rate for lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Hodgkin lymphoma overall 5-year survival is approximately 86%. DLBCL 5-year OS is 60-72% depending on IPI score. Follicular lymphoma median survival exceeds 15 years. Early-stage Hodgkin lymphoma achieves over 95% cure rate with chemotherapy and radiation.

faqs.question: What is R-CHOP for lymphoma?
faqs.answer: R-CHOP is the standard first-line chemotherapy for diffuse large B-cell lymphoma and other aggressive NHL. It combines rituximab (anti-CD20 antibody) with four cytotoxic drugs: cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, vincristine, and prednisone. Six cycles are given over 18 weeks.

faqs.question: Can children get lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Yes. Lymphoma is the third most common childhood cancer. Hodgkin lymphoma peaks in adolescents and young adults. Non-Hodgkin subtypes in children include Burkitt lymphoma, lymphoblastic lymphoma, and DLBCL. Childhood lymphoma has favorable outcomes with intensive chemotherapy.

faqs.question: Is lymphoma hereditary?
faqs.answer: Most lymphomas are not inherited. However, first-degree relatives of CLL and follicular lymphoma patients have about 2-3× the general population risk. Rare inherited syndromes (ataxia-telangiectasia, Wiskott-Aldrich) predispose to lymphoma, but these account for a small fraction of cases.

faqs.question: What is CAR-T therapy for lymphoma?
faqs.answer: CAR-T cell therapy engineers a patient's own T cells to recognize CD19 on lymphoma cells. FDA-approved products include axicabtagene ciloleucel, tisagenlecleucel, and lisocabtagene maraleucel for relapsed DLBCL. About 30-40% of treated patients achieve long-term remission. Main risks are cytokine release syndrome and neurotoxicity.

faqs.question: What is follicular lymphoma?
faqs.answer: Follicular lymphoma is the second most common NHL, an indolent B-cell cancer arising from germinal center B cells. It progresses slowly over many years and responds well to rituximab-based therapy, though most cases relapse. It is not curable with standard treatments but is manageable long-term.

faqs.question: Can lymphoma come back after treatment?
faqs.answer: Yes. Relapse rates depend on subtype: aggressive lymphoma like DLBCL relapses in 30-40% after first-line R-CHOP, usually within 2 years. Hodgkin lymphoma relapses in 10-20%. Follicular lymphoma almost universally relapses but may respond to multiple subsequent regimens over many years.

faqs.question: Does lymphoma cause pain?
faqs.answer: Lymphoma lymph nodes are classically painless. However, bone pain from marrow involvement, abdominal pain from splenomegaly or bulky abdominal nodes, and chest discomfort from mediastinal disease can occur. Alcohol-induced lymph node pain is a rare but classic symptom of Hodgkin lymphoma.

symptomKeywords: swollen lymph nodes neck no infection
metaDescription: Lymphoma: cancer of lymphocytes in lymph nodes. Covers Hodgkin and non-Hodgkin types, R-CHOP treatment, CAR-T therapy, and survival rates, medically reviewed.
